did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper evaluated state-of-the-art machine learning and deep learning algorithms submitted to the 2019 International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) Grand Challenge for the task of skin cancer classification from dermoscopic images. The study quantified the performance of these AI models in diagnosing melanoma and other skin diseases, comparing their accuracy against expert dermatologists.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental results from the 2019 International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) Grand Challenge. It describes a validation study involving the design of a large dermoscopic image classification challenge, the collection and analysis of 25,331 training images and 8,238 test images, the evaluation of 129 algorithm submissions by 64 teams, and a comparative analysis against 18 expert dermatologists. The study includes specific methods, results (e.g., balanced accuracy metrics), and an interpretation of safety issues, which characterizes it as an original research article rather than a review.

title: Validation of artificial intelligence prediction models for skin cancer diagnosis using dermoscopy images: the 2019 International Skin Imaging Collaboration Grand Challenge
brief_summary: This study validated artificial intelligence prediction models for skin cancer diagnosis using dermoscopy images through the 2019 International Skin Imaging Collaboration Grand Challenge. The researchers evaluated 129 algorithm submissions from 64 teams on a test set of 8,238 images, including data from institutions and disease categories not present in the training datasets. The best-performing algorithm achieved 58.8% balanced accuracy on the clinically realistic BCN20000 dataset, a significant drop from the 82.0% accuracy on the HAM10000 benchmark, due to shifted statistical distributions and untrained disease categories. When compared against 18 expert dermatologists, AI algorithms outperformed experts in most categories but failed to safely flag images from untrained diagnostic classes, misclassifying 47.1% of them as malignant. These findings highlight critical safety deficiencies and the need for improved benchmarking protocols that mimic real-world clinical scenarios before AI deployment.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the eight trained diagnostic categories available in the training dataset and test set.

paper_terms: diagnostic classes that were not included in the training data
lesion_types_studied.details: A ninth category in the test dataset containing diagnoses not included in the training data, such as healthy skin, warts, cysts, and benign alterations. Algorithms performed poorly on this class, often misclassifying them as malignant.

all_task_types.target: Diagnostic categories of skin lesions (nevi, melanoma, benign keratosis, dermatofibroma, basal cell carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma, vascular lesions, actinic keratosis, and not trained)
all_task_types.details: The paper evaluates machine learning algorithms for the task of skin cancer classification from dermoscopic images. The challenge involved classifying images into eight trained diagnostic categories and a ninth 'not trained' category. Performance was measured using balanced accuracy and sAUROC. The study also evaluated a secondary task involving the use of clinical metadata alongside images for classification.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: A dermoscopic image dataset collected from the Medical University of Vienna, used as a benchmark for a previous ISIC challenge in 2018. It served as the primary training dataset alongside BCN20000.

use.training: Used as part of the combined training dataset (with BCN20000) for challenge participants to design algorithms.
use.validation: Used as a benchmark test set to evaluate algorithm performance (balanced accuracy of 82.0%).
use.testing: Used as a benchmark test set to evaluate algorithm performance.
use.other: Used as a previously published benchmark for comparison.

datasets_used.name: BCN20000
datasets_used.description: A large public dermoscopic image dataset created by the researchers to simulate realistic clinical scenarios, containing images from Hospital Clinic Barcelona and other institutions (Turkey, New Zealand, Sweden, Argentina). It was designed to better reflect real-world clinical scenarios with shifted statistical distributions and untrained disease categories.

use.training: Used as part of the combined training dataset (with HAM10000) for challenge participants to design algorithms.
use.validation: Used as a test set to evaluate algorithm performance (balanced accuracy of 58.8%).
use.testing: Used as a test set to evaluate algorithm performance on clinically realistic scenarios.
use.other: Designed to evaluate the effects of statistical imbalances, untrained categories, and clinical data quality.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2019 Test Set
datasets_used.description: The official test dataset for the 2019 International Skin Imaging Collaboration Grand Challenge, comprising 8,238 images retrospectively collected from Hospital Clinic Barcelona, Medical University of Vienna, and other countries (Turkey, New Zealand, Sweden, Argentina). It contained all diagnostic categories available in training plus a 'not trained' (NT) category.

use.testing: Used as the primary test set for 129 algorithm submissions from 64 teams.

datasets_used.name: Validation Dataset
datasets_used.description: An independent, unbalanced validation dataset of 100 randomly selected dermoscopic images captured between Jan 1, 2000, and Dec 31, 2018, from the Medical University of Vienna.

use.validation: Available to challenge participants for validation purposes.

datasets_used.name: Expert Reader Study Dataset
datasets_used.description: A subset of 1,269 images selected from the test set pool, used for comparison between AI algorithms and 18 expert dermatologists. Experts classified images in groups of 30.

use.testing: Used for comparative evaluation against 18 expert dermatologists.
use.other: Used to assess safety and real-world accuracy in a simulated clinical setting.

metrics_used_to_evaluate_results: area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUROC)

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To compare algorithms' balanced accuracy performance between data subsets (e.g., BCN with NT vs BCN without NT vs HAM).
To compare algorithm performance on the same images, used because algorithms were evaluated on the same images.
To compare confusion matrices and area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUROC) with imaging and lesion factors, using algorithm identifiers as group labels.
To compare performance between expert readers and algorithms.
Significance threshold/result for the comparison of expert readers vs algorithms on images from categories not included in training data (26% correct for experts vs 6% correct for algorithms).
Significance level for comparisons of median balanced accuracy across all participant submissions for each test set partition.
The defined significance level for all hypothesis tests.

hypothesis_testing.test: Bonferroni-adjusted paired t tests; Paired Student’s t test; Exchangeable covariance matrix for confusion matrices and AUROC; Summary sAUROC metric for expert vs algorithm comparison
hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no difference in balanced accuracy (or other metrics) between the compared groups (e.g., algorithms on different data subsets, or algorithms vs experts).
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a significant difference in balanced accuracy (or other metrics) between the compared groups.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The study used a significance level of 0.05 for all hypothesis tests. Specifically, it reported that algorithms performed significantly worse than expert dermatologists on images from categories not included in training data (26% correct for experts vs 6% correct for algorithms, p<0·0001). It also noted significant differences in median balanced accuracy across test set partitions (p<0·001).

HAM10000.notes: Used as a previously published benchmark. The best algorithm's balanced accuracy reduced by 23.2% (from 82.0% to 58.8%) when comparing HAM to BCN.

BCN20000.notes: Designed to better reflect realistic clinical scenarios with shifted statistical distributions and untrained disease categories. The best algorithm achieved 58.8% balanced accuracy.

BCN20000 (without NT category).notes: Median accuracy across all participant submissions.

BCN20000 (with NT category).notes: Median accuracy across all participant submissions.

HAM (without NT category).notes: Median accuracy across all participant submissions.

HAM (with NT category).notes: Note: The text states 'BCN with NT 0.42' and 'HAM 0.68' in Figure 1A table, but clarifies in text that HAM benchmark was 82.0%. The median values in Fig 1A are for all submissions. The 82.0% refers to the best algorithm on the HAM benchmark partition.

Diagnostic Classes (Top 25 Algorithms -.notes: Median balanced accuracy for each diagnostic class across all submissions.

Expert Reader Study (1269 images).metric: Accuracy (Mean count of correct classifications in batches of 30)
Expert Reader Study (1269 images).notes: Comparison of 18 expert dermatologists vs algorithms on 1269 images. Experts performed significantly better than algorithms on NT images (26% vs 6%, p<0.0001). Top 3 algorithms had better diagnostic accuracy than expert readers on BCC (91% vs 70%), DF (73% vs 50%), and NV (76% vs 56%).

Not Trained (NT) Category (Top 25 Algori.notes: Algorithms failed to safely flag images from untrained diagnostic classes. 47.1% of images from categories not included in training data were misclassified as malignant diagnoses by the top 25 submitted algorithms.

Auxiliary Metadata Impact.notes: Use of auxiliary metadata (lesion anatomic location, patient sex, and age) slightly improved mean algorithm accuracy.

architecture_used.main_model: Multiple state-of-the-art machine learning and deep learning algorithms submitted by 64 teams; specific architectures not uniformly reported, but EfficientNet and ResNet were used by top-performing teams.
architecture_used.pretraining: Not explicitly reported for all submissions; however, EfficientNet and ResNet are typically pretrained on ImageNet in similar contexts, but this specific detail is not confirmed in the text for all models.

architecture_details.top_performing_architecture: Ensembles of the EfficientNet architecture (used by the top two performing algorithms).
architecture_details.other_approaches: Direct 8-class models allowing the image not to belong to any class, and Shannon entropy estimation.

training_details.training_data: 25,331 training images from HAM10000 and BCN20000 datasets.
training_details.metadata_usage: Some algorithms used auxiliary metadata (lesion anatomic location, patient sex, and age).
training_details.external_data: The best performing team trained a model on external data obtained by challenge participants.

classification_or_output_head.output_classes: Eight trained diagnostic categories (nevi, melanoma, benign keratosis, dermatofibroma, basal cell carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma, vascular lesions, actinic keratosis) and one 'not trained' (NT) category.
classification_or_output_head.safety_mechanism: Algorithms were tested on their ability to fail safely by correctly identifying diagnostic categories on which they were not trained (NT category).

preprocessing_techniques: The paper does not report specific preprocessing techniques (e.g., resizing, normalization, hair removal) applied by the challenge participants, as it evaluates 129 diverse algorithm submissions from 64 teams.
The study notes that challenge participants were free to design appropriate algorithms, implying they applied their own preprocessing steps.

The researchers manually annotated imaging features and artifacts (ulceration, crust, pigmentation, hair, pen marks) for analysis, but this was for evaluation purposes, not a reported preprocessing step for the AI models.
conclusions: State-of-the-art AI algorithms for skin cancer diagnosis exhibit significant safety deficiencies and reduced accuracy in clinically realistic scenarios compared to previous benchmark studies.

The best-performing algorithm achieved 58.8% balanced accuracy on the BCN20000 dataset, which simulates realistic clinical scenarios with shifted statistical distributions and untrained disease categories, a significant drop from the 82.0% accuracy achieved on the HAM10000 benchmark.
Shifted statistical distributions and disease categories not included in the training data were primary contributors to the decrease in classification accuracy.
Image artifacts (hair, pen markings, ulceration) and imaging source institution decreased accuracy in a complex manner that varied based on the underlying diagnosis.
When compared to 18 expert dermatologists, AI algorithms outperformed experts in most diagnostic categories but failed to safely flag images from categories not included in the training data.
For images from categories not included in training data, expert dermatologists achieved 26% correct classification compared to only 6% for algorithms (p<0.0001).

Among the top 25 submitted algorithms, 47.1% of images from categories not included in training data were misclassified as malignant diagnoses, which would lead to a substantial number of unnecessary biopsies if current state-of-the-art AI technologies were clinically deployed.
The use of auxiliary clinical metadata (lesion anatomic location, patient sex, and age) slightly improved mean algorithm accuracy (from 50% to 56%).
The top two performing algorithms used ensembles of the EfficientNet architecture, while the third-place team used ensembles of the ResNet architecture.

The study identifies specific deficiencies and safety issues in AI diagnostic systems that must be addressed in future diagnostic evaluation protocols to improve safety and reliability in clinical practice.
important_remarks: Significant performance drop in clinically realistic scenarios: The best-performing algorithm's balanced accuracy decreased from 82.0% on the HAM10000 benchmark to 58.8% on the BCN20000 dataset, which simulates real-world conditions with shifted statistical distributions and untrained disease categories.

Critical safety deficiency with untrained categories: Algorithms failed to safely flag images from diagnostic classes not included in training data (NT category), misclassifying 47.1% of these benign or non-target lesions as malignant diagnoses, which would lead to unnecessary biopsies if deployed clinically.

Inability to recognize unknown diseases: While algorithms outperformed expert dermatologists in most trained categories, they performed significantly worse than experts on the NT category (6% correct for algorithms vs. 26% for experts, p<0.0001), highlighting a lack of 'fail-safe' mechanisms for out-of-distribution data.

Impact of image artifacts and source institution: Accuracy was significantly affected by image artifacts (hair, pen markings, ulceration) and the imaging source institution, demonstrating that performance is not robust to variations in image quality and clinical setting.

Limited generalizability due to dataset biases: The study notes limitations regarding the retrospective nature of image collection, scarce diversity in ethnicities, absence of skin tone labeling, and the use of static images that do not fully mimic a dynamic clinical setting.

Small benefit from auxiliary metadata: The use of auxiliary clinical metadata (lesion anatomic location, patient sex, and age) only slightly improved mean algorithm accuracy (from 50% to 56%), suggesting limited utility of available metadata in current models.

Benchmarking protocol concerns: Previous studies often reported high accuracy on controlled datasets (like HAM10000) but failed to assess behavior on shifted distributions or untrained categories, leading to overestimation of real-world clinical utility.
